comment_content:
COMP 4331 disappoints in content delivery. Rather than diving deeply into core data mining concepts (e.g., apriori, k-means, hierarchical clustering), it skims a broad range of topics superficially, leaving gaps in understanding.

Exams span too many areas with no practice or past papers available, making revision tough especially for someone who it terrible at memorizing random things. Lecture notes are poorly designed: overly vague slides often require YouTube supplements to grasp basics, and many exam questions appear unrelated to the material covered.

comment_content:
This is the first 4000 level COMP course I take. And honestly, words cannot describe how disappointed I am to the content of this course. This was a 4000 level COMP course so I was expecting the course to be difficult. I was expecting a lot of math, going in depth into data mining algorithms, stuff like that. But this course shows me that the 4 is just a number.
There are a few problems with the course content. First of all, there's just too little content. I planned out my study schedule and I would skip lectures and study 3 hours for every course per week. This works for other courses. I would usually spend the full 3 hours every week and be able to catch up on the content.
But for this course, only one and a half hour is needed. The content is so little its only 1.5 credits worth. Which is ridiculous considering this is a 3 credit course.

Second problem with this course is how little depth it has. I mean what he taught for each of the algorithms are just scratching the surface. I have taken COMP2211 and gotten A+. And basically the content of naiive bayes, mlp, cnn are taught in much less detail than COMP2211. I actually just recalled my knowledge from COMP2211 for the exams. I learned nothing new. I mean I actually don't know why he included rnn in the syllabus. It's actually funny because rnn is such a complex algorithm and bro just has one slide per gate (output gate, input gate), and each slides shows a graph and a formula. And doesn't explain anything. And then rnn was never in the exam. I mean what's the point of including rnn in the content then if you are not actually gonna teach it and test it.

Another issue is with the slides. Some slides are literally incomprehensible. There's a slide on edge betweenness, talking about an algorithm that measures edge betweenness. To this day, I have no idea what that slide is talking about. AI was suggesting its the Brande's algorithm? But the word "Brande's algorithm" never appeared on the slides. And I have no idea what the slides is talking about whatsoever. I spent at least 5 hours on that slide I think and I gave up at the end. Guess what? It appeared in the exam. Awesome. And for reference its probably not because I am dumb because I got A in comp3711 just reading the slides.

I have attached some slides as reference. Btw this is the first time I saw filler slides. Just some slides that are not relevant to the content (You can check the community slides). I just thought it was really funny.
comment_teaching:
Disclaimer: What I write here only comments on the slides. I have not even attended a full lecture of James Kwok so I would not comment on how he teach. (I was so bored I left the first lecture after half an hour in front of the whole class)
This is my third year in UST. And honestly I have seen a lot of professors. There are very passionate and helpful professors, those who handwrites their notes, and those whose teaching quality is questionable. But I have never doubted the professionalism of these professors. I thought that each professor must have been one of the top scholars in the world to be able to secure their spot as a professor in a world renowned university.
But from James Kwok's slides, I just don't see it. In the neural network related topics, I actually just doubted if he actually understood the topic himself. He either doesn't know what he is doing, or he just don't care about teaching so much he made such crap slides. I believe the reason would be the latter? But the the slides for the other topics are better. So I don't know what's going on. He literally has so little content in his course. If he actually go in depth to teach any of the topics, he could easily double the content he has in this course and make it a normal comp course workload.
if you compare the neural network slides to the comp2211 neural network slides, you would realise comp2211 probably has around 3-4 times the content of this course. I thought the other topics like decision tree, hierachical clustering, association analysis slides are better (but also very brief ofc), but my friend said comp1942 taught them in way more detail. If this course is worse than a 1000 and 2000 level comp course, should it be 0 level instead? :) Comp0331 lol.
comment_workload:
The homework is probably the only good thing about this course. There are 3 assignments in total, with the first and the second being coding assignments and the third one is just a written assignment. I think its not too challenging and you get to build something with pytorch so its good. The tutorials basically teaches you how to code your assignments and I heard that its ok. The content in the course is of course very manageable as well, since its only about 1.5 credits worth of content.

I am just gonna make a conclusion for my long ass review. So basically I learned very little and left with a bad grade. DO NOT make the same mistake as me. I advice against anyone who wants to take comp4331 when James Kwok is teaching.
comment_grading:
HAHAHAHAHAHA worst grading I have experienced. WOW!!!
The weightings are as follows:
Assignment 1 8%
Assignment 2 9%
Assignment 3 8%
Midterm 25%
Final 50%
Only mean and standard deviation of the exams are given. So here are the statistics:
Midterm exam: Mean->54.5 SD->22.3
Final exam: Mean->58.5 SD->19

My mark:
Assignments nearly full
Midterm: 84 +1.3sd
Final 72.75 +0.75 sd
I mean overall I should be around +0.93sd right? If this was any good grade normal comp course, my mark would be between A- or A. 0.93sd should be top 18% in a normal distribution.
Guess what grade I got. B+. I GOT B+ SCORING +0.93SD. Wow. What can I say. Apparently I am 0.2 away from the A- cutoff. HAHAHA. Amazing.

But after calming down and thinking about it once again. I think there's two reasons why I got B+. First one being that this course does not have a good grading. James Kwok maybe just doesn't give a lot of A range.
But the second reason is probably because the distribution is not normal. For those who are more sensitive towards math, a near 20 sd is not normal at all. In fact, I think the distribution would be close to a bimodal distribution. When going to paper checking, I saw many people who got 80 something in both midterm and final, and also a lot of them getting very low marks like 20 30 something. And this is just really weird to me. It also hints on the teaching quality of James Kwok. If a professor teaches well most student should get a mark close to mean. Just saying.

The midterm was pretty easy. It's just telling you to run algorithms, no trick questions. Nothing testing your concepts or math. Honestly I thought the mean would be like 6X so I was shocked initially. The final exam tests way more in depth than what the slides is. It's almost like he is deliberately putting things that are not on the slides so that less people would know how to do the exam. I only survived because I have a very strong foundation in COMP2211 (Thanks Desmond).

comment_content:
1. Intro: Explain the importance, source and types of data; What analysis could be performed on them (how to mine them); Applications.
2. Knowing your data: a. Statistical description of the data: from median to Q-Q plot; b. Proximity Measures: how similar/dissimilar your datum is compared to other data in different cases(i.e. types of data)
3. Data preprocessing: Data cleaning: Data integration; Data reduction (dimensionality reduction); Data transformation
4. Data warehouse: OLAP vs OLTP: from structure to operations; Data indexing
5. Introduction to classification: types of classification, measurement  of data (confusion matrix, F-measures); Selection of Training and Testing sets
6. Decision tree: Algorithm, Purity and Overfitting handling
7. Bayesian classification: Assumptions, Formulas, Zero probability problem
8. K-nearest neighbour: Algorithm, Choosing the distance measure and K, limitation
9. Regression: Linear and Logistic regression: from formula to purpose; introduction to gradient decent
10. Artificial Neural Networks: From perceptron to propagation algorithm
11. Convolutional Neural Network: Structure and properties
12. Recurrent Neural Network: Introducing cell, attention mechanism to Neural Networks
comment_teaching:
Cecia is one of the best professor I've ever met here in UST in my opinion. Aside from a well-designed teaching plan and a lecture with appropriate grammar, tone and fluency, the mode of delivery includes introducing the logic behind each of the formulas, and when/how/where to use them. Paired with concrete examples on each of the topics. Overall it provides a memorable experience in both understanding and application.

On the flipped side, although TAs are usable along with the tutorial slides, I would rather read the transcript of the recordings if there's any. I will be surprised if those TAs could pass paper 4.
comment_workload:
HW: Coding in python, 6+ hours per each PA even with pandas experience & the help from chat-GPT.

Revision:An additional 1.5h for each lecture for reviewing.

Kind of gross if you ask me, but... your choice.

P.S. I'm not a CS student. If you have had enough of them taking over your territory, it is time to take revenge.
P.S.S. There's another review written by a different student regarding the same class, the information on that one is accurate while being more specific.

comment_teaching:
Prof Kwok focus on the algorithm on data mining, which is quite different from what I expect he will teach in the course. In fact, he only talks about the algorithm, and in his lectures he never talk about any coding which is related to data mining. However, in the assignment and project later, require advanced techniques which make me suffer when doing the assignments and projects. Although TA will talk about coding in tutorial section, it is not sufficient because there is just one hour per week and they just talked about very easy codes. when it come to assignments and projects, it still take tons of time for me to google and find out what code is useful.

Unlike other comp course, it does not have lab session. I would prefer there is one though the workload will be more.  The reason is lab session can help consolidate and make application what we learnt in the lecture and give more ideas when it comes to assignment and projects.
comment_workload: Workload is huge. As mentioned above, you need to find out what code is useful in your assignment and projects. You will not know what code you can use if you just attend the lecture even the tutorials. Project arrangement is also very bad. Group formation deadline is 30/11 this semester. He did not take into account that many students are having final in early to mid December. Assignment 3 is also due in mid December as well. As a result, in December, I needed to revise for my finals, do assignment 3 and group project which is a disaster. It is not understandable that why he did not set the deadline earlier and give students more time to do the project.

comment_content: The content is mainly practical machine learning. The course mainly focuses on applying machine learning techniques using python and several of its libraries. Course content includes classification, frequent item mining, social media mining, web mining, etc. This course focuses on the practical aspect of machine learning unlike the machine learning course in HKUST, which focuses more on the theoretical aspect of machine learning.
comment_teaching: The course will be difficult to understand as the slides are structured very inefficiently. He covers the content in a random order so the order of the lecture slides won't matter much. His english is ok but hard to understand some parts. Searching online and reading articles about the topic helped me a ton when I didn't understand class lectures.
comment_workload: The workload will be very tough as there are 3 big assignments as well as a big project that is announced early on. As the course covers a lot of content, studying for the exams will also be rough. The assignments are doable but require lots of checking and waiting as running the machine learning code will often take hours to complete. The group project also requires a lot of effort as the professor expects us to use most of the content covered in the course without much help from the tutors. A lot of individual research on the web is required.

The grading was very good considering the percentage I got.

Midterm 42/60, mean 37
Final 78/100, mean 75
Grade: A-

The projects and assignments are each 30% and 20%, and midterm is 20% and final is 30%. Although the content is difficult and project is very demanding, the grades turned out to be good.

comment_content: The content of the course is not bad. This course mainly focuses on data mining. To be more specific, it is about frequent item mining, classification methods, clustering methods, collaborative filter, social media mining and web mining. If you fully understand the concept of the course, you will definitely have a clear understanding on data mining and machine learning.
comment_teaching: I have to say Prof. Chen's teaching is awful. Initially, I think that he is a good instructor. However, after going through half of the semester, I am totally confused about the concepts and I cannot learn any essential information from the lecture. At the same time, the lectures and tutorials are not related to the course assignment and project AT ALL. Especially in November, he just went through the lecture slides in a extremely fast pace. comment_workload:
Very significant workload. The project and assignments all requires significant time to complete.

Since this is a "machine learning" course, the running time of the computer program takes extremely long, which means that you have to wait for a really long time to determine whether your code yields the right result. At the same time, there is no skeleton code for all the assignments and project, which means that you have to start from the very beginning. comment_content:
An "introductory" course to data mining and machine learning. Just like any other "introductory" courses, if you have no prior knowledge you'll surely be fucked up. If you're RMBI students or you aren't proficient in Python, DROP THIS COURSE IMMEDIATELY and take ISOM3360 instead.

Key topics include a number of algorithms for frequent pattern mining, classification, clustering, outlier detection, collaborative filtering, etc.

0️⃣ Introduction to Data Mining
1️⃣ Getting to know your data
2️⃣ Data Preprocessing
3️⃣ Frequent Pattern Mining
      🔶 Apriori
      🔶 FP-growth
      🔶 Discretize Continuous Variables
      🔶 Generalized Sequential Pattern (GSP)
4️⃣ Classification
      🔶 K-nearest Neighbor (KNN)
      🔶 Decision Tree Induction
      🔶 Naive Bayes Classifier
      🔶 Bayesian Belief Network
      🔶 Confusion Matrix
      🔶 Ensemble Methods
      🔶 Neural Network
      🔶 Support Vector Machines (SVM)
5️⃣ Clustering
      🔶 K-means Clustering
      🔶 K-medoids Clustering
            PAM
      🔶 Hierarchical Clustering
      🔶 Density-based Clustering
            DBSCAN
      🔶 Fuzzy Clustering
            Fuzzy EM
      🔶 Probabilistic Model-based Clustering
            Univariate Gaussian Mixture Model
      🔶 Subspace Clustering
            Dense Unit-based and Entropy-based Methods
6️⃣ Outlier Analysis
      🔶 Statistical Approaches
      🔶 Nested Loop Algorithm
      🔶 Cell-based Algorithm
      🔶 Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
7️⃣ Collaborative Filtering
8️⃣ Social Media
      🔶 Edge Betweenness
      🔶 K-L
9️⃣ OLAP and Data Cube
      🔶 OLAP Operations
      🔶 Data Warehouse: Materialize View
            Greedy Algorithm
🔟 Web Mining
      🔶 PageRank
      🔶 Hypertext-Induced Topic Search (HITS)

Note that this course is mainly on theory and is grossly unrelated to the assignments and the project. Nobody ever teaches how to code the algorithm and how to use the Python packages. When it comes to the assignments and the project, we all end up in learning all by ourselves.

I sincerely wish that we were taught how to import, manipulate, and visualize data using the NumPy, pandas, matplotlib and scikit-learn packages. Knowing how to do data mining by hand doesn't help sh!t at all.
comment_teaching:
At first Prof CHEN's teaching seemed not bad at all. But I later discovered that he had a notorious habit of just skimming through all these algorithms and casually skipping a lot of the details, and he expected all of us to understand all of them as if we were all genii? I knew there were a lot of genii in the class, but I was deffo not one of them. Thus I instead learned by going over YouTube videos on data mining, and I found this 100x more effective than attending his lectures.

Okay. Just another self-learning course.
comment_workload:
Extremely heavy workload. Prof Chen seems to have very high expectation on us.

💻 20% Assignments
💻 30% Project
✏️ 20% Midterm
✏️ 30% Final

There are a total of 3 assignments in Python. All of them require the use of Anaconda, a distribution of Python designed for the purpose of data mining. The assignments are deffo not easy and we are required to spend some time to self-explore what the heck the tasks are and how to use the scikit-learn packages. Again, it's about the ability to self-learn.

And then the fucking project! It was my worst nightmare ever. The project was a dropout prediction task extracted from the professional KDD-cup competition. We were allowed to form groups of up to 3 groupmates, but even with 3 people we still ate sh!t in the project. The project required us to do the data preprocessing (which spent us about 80% of our time) including loading and manipulating the data into a data structure we needed, feature engineering with discretization, data aggregation and transformation, selecting 3 classifiers for prediction, applying cross-validation for these classifiers, ensembling the classifiers and evaluating the classifiers with confusion matrices. This is the most demanding project I've ever met and you can imagine all my groupmate staying in front of our PCs days and nights coding and debugging and coding and debugging... An excellent opportunity to get hands-on experience of how it feels in the hell.

As for the exams they're basically concept-based written papers covering various algorithms taught in class. No codes. Just data mining by hands, with a definitely insufficient amount of time. They were among the sh!ttiest papers ever as they differentiated us based on our speed instead of our understanding of the concepts.
comment_grading:
Not bad. The TAs have been quite lenient in marking our assignments and projects. But as most of us score high in the assignments and the projects, exams become the determining factor of our grade.

✏️ Midterm
Mean = 37/60

✏️ Final
Mean = 75/100, SD = 15

I got 36/60 in the midterm, 78/100 in the final, 98/100 in the project and averaged around 90 in the 3 assignments. Overall I got around 82.7% and this translated into a B+.

As a tip, don't be in despair when your codes do not run. I encountered quite a few times the situation when the codes in my assignments and project did not run at all, but I still managed to secure 90-ish by outlining the logic in my comments, explaining how I approached the problem, writing some partial codes, and most importantly, submitting PUNCTUALLY. Don't submit late even if you cannot finish the assignments. You might be surprised at how many marks you've secured by submitting partial codes with some explanation in the code and the report. Another thing is that if you've used Python packages outside of those suggested by the TAs (which is totally fine), there is no way the TAs could verify whether your codes could actually run or not.

comment_workload:
As this course is more focused on concepts it is important that you need to understand them well. The exam questions are designed so that you need to think more rather than recalling the concepts you've memorized.
The exams are closed book so you still need to memorize some basic formula for calculating something, but that is minimal.
comment_grading:
The grading is pretty generous and he is quite flexible in discussing with us the exam coverage and assignments.

The assignments are mostly pencil and paper problems, with one question related to programming. But even for programming it is more focused on applying the concepts you've learned in class so it 's not too difficult and as long as you know how to program in Java or C++ it's pretty easy.

comment_content:
Important concepts of data mining and big data are covered well. Good introductory course if you are curious in big data (how come diapers and beers are often bought together on a supermarket?) or looking to be a data scientist like me.
Assignments cover both written and programming (1st assignment in c++ while 2nd one in java or c++). Then midterm and final as usual.
The course was more on understanding the concepts than mathematics which I was expecting. You would find more questions using mathematics on the assignments while the exams would have more conceptual questions (like predicting how the result would look like given this input and this technique then explaining why).
comment_teaching:
Lectures are okay. Sometimes he tries to make jokes and when I get it I laugh (when I don't get it I just smile). But when he goes really technical, it could be rather boring. Sometimes he tries to show live demos of the materials over websites but mostly fails because the demo is using Java Applet. The lecture desktop does not allow it to run.
One time he was demoing a concept by googling his name. Somehow he stumbled to a website containing reviews about his teachings. He was surprised and spent a minute reading the review in front of the class. That was the best lecture session of this course in my opinion.
comment_workload:
Assignment workload is reasonable (3 assignments). Usually not taking too much time compared to other COMP4xxx courses.
If your programming skills aren't that good or you just don't like programming, it might take more time for you to do the programming assignment. Usually you need to implement one of the machine learning techniques (the easy ones). You should also make some documentation about the program (comments on code, how to run, etc.).
comment_grading: Seems the course is quite generous in putting the A range. Just try to keep your grades on almost 1 SD and above mean and you should be guaranteed A range I guess.
